However, to be safe, I will ensure "build confidence" appears *only* as part of that specific required phrase.

    *   *Drafting text:*
        "Public speaking is often cited as one of the most common fears people face. Whether you are presenting a report to your colleagues, giving a toast at a wedding, or speaking at a large conference, the sensation of a racing heart and sweaty palms can be overwhelming. However, with the right approach, this anxiety can be managed and even channeled into a powerful performance.

        The first step in overcoming this hurdle is thorough preparation. Anxiety often stems from a fear of the unknown or a worry that you will forget your points. By mastering your material, you create a safety net for your mind. Practice your speech multiple times—not just in your head, but out loud. Record yourself or present to a small group of friends to get comfortable with the flow of your words.

        Physical techniques are equally important. When the adrenaline hits, your breathing often becomes shallow. Practicing deep, diaphragmatic breathing can signal to your nervous system that you are safe. Additionally, focusing on your posture can make a significant difference. Standing tall with your shoulders back not only improves your vocal projection but also helps you feel more grounded.

        Another effective method is cognitive reframing. Instead of telling yourself that you are nervous, try telling yourself that you are excited. Physiologically, nervousness and excitement feel very similar; by changing the label you give the sensation, you can shift your mindset from fear to enthusiasm.

        To truly master the stage, it is helpful to focus on practical strategies to build confidence in public speaking. These include visualizing a successful outcome, focusing on the value you are providing to the audience, and accepting that minor mistakes are perfectly normal.

        Ultimately, public speaking is a skill that improves with repetition. The more you do it, the more your comfort level will grow. By taking small steps and applying these techniques, you can turn your anxiety into a tool for effective communication."

    How to overcome public speaking anxiety

    Public speaking is one of the most common fears people face. Whether you are presenting a report to your colleagues, giving a toast at a wedding, or speaking at a large conference, the sensation of a racing heart and sweaty palms can be overwhelming. However, with the right approach, this anxiety can be managed and even channeled into a powerful performance.

    The first step in overcoming this hurdle is thorough preparation. Anxiety often stems from a fear of the unknown or a worry that you will forget your points. By mastering your material, you create a safety net for your mind. Practice your speech multiple times—not just in your head, but out loud. Record yourself or present to a small group of friends to get comfortable with the flow of your words.

    Physical techniques are equally important. When the adrenaline hits, your breathing often becomes shallow. Practicing deep, diaphragmatic breathing can signal to your nervous system that you are safe. Additionally, focusing on your posture can make a significant difference. Standing tall with your shoulders back not only improves your vocal projection but also helps you feel more grounded.

    Another effective method is cognitive reframing. Instead of telling yourself that you are nervous, try telling yourself that you are excited. Physiologically, nervousness and excitement feel very similar; by changing the label you give the sensation, you can shift your mindset from fear to enthusiasm.

    To truly master the stage, it is helpful to focus on practical strategies to build confidence in public speaking. These include visualizing a successful outcome, focusing on the value you are providing to the audience, and accepting that minor mistakes are perfectly normal.

    Ultimately, public speaking is a skill that improves with repetition. The more you do it, the more your comfort level will grow. By taking small steps and applying these techniques, you can turn your anxiety into a tool for effective communication.
